---
title: "Ch 3a — Trigonometric Functions (Radians, Unit Circle, Graphs)"
subtitle: "Trigonometry escapes the right triangle and runs along the whole real line"
lesson-id: "cbse11.trig-functions"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 3
ncert-chapter-title: "Trigonometric Functions"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse10.trigonometric-identities", "t3.unit-circle"]
unlocks: ["cbse11.trig-identities-compound-angles", "cbse11.limits", "cbse12.continuity"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you state $\sin 30^\circ$, $\cos 60^\circ$, $\tan 45^\circ$ from
   memory?
2. Do you remember the right-triangle definitions: opposite over
   hypotenuse, adjacent over hypotenuse, etc.?
3. Have you drawn a unit circle (radius 1, centre at origin)?
4. Can you identify the four quadrants of the coordinate plane?

Three confident yeses and you're ready. Otherwise, refresh
**[Class 10 Trigonometric Identities](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/08b-trigonometric-identities/index.qmd)**
or **[Unit Circle](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/unit-circle/index.qmd)**.
:::

## Touch

Take a piece of string of length exactly the radius of a small circle
you can draw. Pin one end at the centre. Lay the string along the
circle's circumference, starting from the point where the circle meets
the positive $x$-axis. Mark where the string ends.

The angle from the positive $x$-axis to that mark is **one radian** —
the angle that an arc of length one radius subtends at the centre.
There is nothing special about degrees; radians are nature's choice
because the arc length and the angle measure agree.

The widget below lets you drag a point around the unit circle. Watch
how the angle (in radians and degrees) and the coordinates change
together.

```{ojs}
viewof theta = Inputs.range([0, 2 * Math.PI], {value: Math.PI / 4, step: 0.01, label: "Angle θ (radians)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const x = Math.cos(theta).toFixed(3);
  const y = Math.sin(theta).toFixed(3);
  const deg = (theta * 180 / Math.PI).toFixed(1);
  const tan = Math.abs(Math.cos(theta)) > 0.001 ? (Math.sin(theta) / Math.cos(theta)).toFixed(3) : "undefined";
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>Angle:</b> ${theta.toFixed(3)} rad = ${deg}°</div>
    <div><b>Point on unit circle:</b> (cos θ, sin θ) = (${x}, ${y})</div>
    <div><b>tan θ = sin θ / cos θ =</b> ${tan}</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.5em;color:#666;">
      Notice: cos θ is the x-coordinate, sin θ is the y-coordinate. Always.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

This is the unit-circle definition. It is *the* definition for the
rest of this curriculum.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. If $\sin\theta$ is the $y$-coordinate of a point on the unit circle,
   what is the largest value it can take? The smallest? Can it equal
   $2$?
2. $\sin 0 = 0$. Where else on $[0, 2\pi]$ is $\sin\theta = 0$? Why
   exactly there?
3. As $\theta$ grows past $2\pi$, the point comes back to where it
   started. What does this say about the function $\sin$?
4. The right-triangle definition seemed to require a triangle with a
   right angle inside it. Where does the triangle go when $\theta$ is,
   say, $200^\circ$?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) reveals that sine and cosine are bounded between $-1$ and $1$ — a
fact you'll lean on for the rest of trigonometry. (3) is **periodicity**;
(4) is the whole point of switching to the unit circle.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Radians and degrees

Convert $60°$ to radians, and $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ radians to degrees.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$180° = \pi$ radians. So $1° = \pi/180$ rad and $1$ rad $= 180/\pi$ deg.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$60° = 60 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ radians.

$\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ rad $= \dfrac{3\pi}{4} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 135°$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Sin and cos for special angles

State the values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ for
$\theta = 0, \pi/6, \pi/4, \pi/3, \pi/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The "$1, \sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, 2$ over $2$" pattern.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| $\theta$ | $0$ | $\pi/6$ | $\pi/4$ | $\pi/3$ | $\pi/2$ |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| $\sin\theta$ | $0$ | $1/2$ | $\sqrt{2}/2$ | $\sqrt{3}/2$ | $1$ |
| $\cos\theta$ | $1$ | $\sqrt{3}/2$ | $\sqrt{2}/2$ | $1/2$ | $0$ |

Memorise this table by Friday. It will save you minutes on every test
for the next three years.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Signs in each quadrant

In which quadrants is $\sin\theta$ positive? In which is $\cos\theta$
negative? In which is $\tan\theta$ negative?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin$ is the $y$-coordinate, $\cos$ is the $x$-coordinate. Look at
the signs of $x$ and $y$ in each quadrant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Quadrant | $\sin\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | $\tan\theta$ |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| I | + | + | + |
| II | + | − | − |
| III | − | − | + |
| IV | − | + | − |

Mnemonic: **A**ll, **S**ilver, **T**ea, **C**ups (or "All Students Take
Calculus") — quadrants I, II, III, IV: All positive, Sin only, Tan
only, Cos only.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Out of range angles

What is $\sin\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{6}\right)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\dfrac{7\pi}{6} = \pi + \dfrac{\pi}{6}$. The angle is in quadrant III.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Reference angle $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$, in quadrant III where sine is
negative. So $\sin\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{6}\right) = -\dfrac{1}{2}$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Periodicity

Compute $\sin(2\pi + \pi/3)$ and $\cos(4\pi - \pi/4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Both $\sin$ and $\cos$ have period $2\pi$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin(2\pi + \pi/3) = \sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$.

$\cos(4\pi - \pi/4) = \cos(-\pi/4) = \cos(\pi/4) = \sqrt{2}/2$
(cosine is even).
</details>

### Problem 6 — Pythagorean identity

Verify $\sin^2(\pi/3) + \cos^2(\pi/3) = 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$, $\cos(\pi/3) = 1/2$.

$\left(\sqrt{3}/2\right)^2 + (1/2)^2 = 3/4 + 1/4 = 1$. ✓

This is a special case of $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$, which is
just $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ for points on the unit circle.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Domain of tangent

For which real $\theta$ is $\tan\theta$ undefined? Why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\tan\theta = \sin\theta / \cos\theta$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Undefined when $\cos\theta = 0$, i.e. $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$
for any integer $n$. At those angles $\sin\theta = \pm 1$ and division
blows up.
</details>

You have just used **radians**, the **unit-circle definitions**,
**quadrant signs**, **reference angles**, **periodicity**, the
**Pythagorean identity**, and the **domain of tangent**. Now we name
them.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For any real number $\theta$, let $P(\theta) = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$
be the point on the unit circle reached by rotating the point $(1, 0)$
through angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise.

The six trigonometric functions are
$$\sin\theta,\ \cos\theta,\ \tan\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta},$$
$$\csc\theta = \frac{1}{\sin\theta},\ \sec\theta = \frac{1}{\cos\theta},\ \cot\theta = \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}.$$

**Pythagorean identities:**
$$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1, \quad 1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta, \quad 1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta.$$

**Periodicity:** $\sin, \cos, \sec, \csc$ have period $2\pi$;
$\tan, \cot$ have period $\pi$.

**Symmetry:** $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$ (odd),
$\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$ (even).

**Conversion:** $180° = \pi$ rad. Multiply by $\pi/180$ to go deg → rad.

:::

A short verse:

> *Round the unit circle the angle does ride;*
> *Cosine is across, sine is up the side.*
> *Square them and add — you get one every time;*
> *Two pi is the song, then the tune starts to chime.*

**Graphs to memorise:**

- $y = \sin x$: smooth wave, range $[-1, 1]$, zeros at $n\pi$, peaks
  at $\pi/2 + 2n\pi$.
- $y = \cos x$: same wave shifted left by $\pi/2$.
- $y = \tan x$: vertical asymptotes at $\pi/2 + n\pi$, zeros at $n\pi$,
  passes through $(0, 0)$ with slope $1$.

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find $\sin\theta$ and $\tan\theta$ if $\cos\theta = -3/5$ and $\theta$
> lies in quadrant II.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Use the Pythagorean identity to find $\sin\theta$:
   $\sin^2\theta = 1 - 9/25 = 16/25$, so $\sin\theta = \pm 4/5$.
2. Quadrant II: $\sin > 0$, so $\sin\theta = 4/5$.
3. $\tan\theta = \sin\theta / \cos\theta = (4/5) / (-3/5) = -4/3$.

**Answers:** $\sin\theta = 4/5$, $\tan\theta = -4/3$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Convert $225°$ to radians.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$225 \times \pi/180 = 5\pi/4$.
</details>

**W2.** State $\cos(7\pi/6)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Quadrant III, reference $\pi/6$, cosine negative: $-\sqrt{3}/2$.
</details>

**W3.** Find the period of $\sin(3x)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin(kx)$ has period $2\pi/k$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Period $= 2\pi/3$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** If $\tan\theta = 4/3$ and $\theta$ is in quadrant III, find
$\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Build a 3-4-5 triangle, then assign signs by quadrant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan = 4/3$ → opposite $4$, adjacent $3$, hypotenuse $5$. In Q III both
sin and cos are negative: $\sin\theta = -4/5$, $\cos\theta = -3/5$.
</details>

**P2.** Prove $\sin^4 x - \cos^4 x = \sin^2 x - \cos^2 x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Difference of squares: $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$. Then use $\sin^2 x +
\cos^2 x = 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LHS $= (\sin^2 x)^2 - (\cos^2 x)^2 = (\sin^2 x - \cos^2 x)(\sin^2 x +
\cos^2 x) = (\sin^2 x - \cos^2 x)(1) = $ RHS.
</details>

**P3.** Find all the trig values for $\theta = -5\pi/6$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$-5\pi/6$ is the same point as $-5\pi/6 + 2\pi = 7\pi/6$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin(-5\pi/6) = -1/2$, $\cos(-5\pi/6) = -\sqrt{3}/2$,
$\tan(-5\pi/6) = 1/\sqrt{3}$, and reciprocals follow.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that $\dfrac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} =
\dfrac{\sin\theta}{1 + \cos\theta}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply numerator and denominator on one side by $1 + \cos\theta$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{1 - \cos\theta}{\sin\theta} \cdot \dfrac{1+\cos\theta}{1+\cos\theta}
= \dfrac{1 - \cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta (1+\cos\theta)} =
\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\sin\theta(1+\cos\theta)} =
\dfrac{\sin\theta}{1+\cos\theta}$.
</details>

**S2.** Find the maximum and minimum of $f(\theta) = 3\sin\theta +
4\cos\theta$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Write $a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta = R\sin(\theta + \phi)$ where
$R = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$R = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$. So $f = 5\sin(\theta + \phi)$ for some $\phi$,
giving max $= 5$, min $= -5$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** $\sin(\pi - x)$ equals
(a) $-\sin x$ (b) $\sin x$ (c) $\cos x$ (d) $-\cos x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi - x$ has the same $y$-coordinate as $x$ (reflection across $y$-axis).
**Answer: (b) $\sin x$.**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the value of
$\sin\dfrac{31\pi}{3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Subtract multiples of $2\pi$ until the angle is in $[0, 2\pi)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{31\pi}{3} = \dfrac{31\pi}{3} - 10\pi = \dfrac{31\pi - 30\pi}{3}
= \dfrac{\pi}{3}$.

So $\sin\dfrac{31\pi}{3} = \sin\dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** If $\cos x = -\dfrac{12}{13}$ and $x$ is
in quadrant II, find the values of all six trigonometric functions.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pythagorean identity for $\sin x$, then divide.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin^2 x = 1 - 144/169 = 25/169$. Q II: $\sin x > 0$, so
$\sin x = 5/13$.

$\tan x = -5/12$, $\csc x = 13/5$, $\sec x = -13/12$,
$\cot x = -12/5$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** (i) Prove $\dfrac{\cos\theta}{1-\sin\theta} +
\dfrac{\cos\theta}{1+\sin\theta} = 2\sec\theta$.
(ii) Hence find $\dfrac{\cos(\pi/3)}{1-\sin(\pi/3)} +
\dfrac{\cos(\pi/3)}{1+\sin(\pi/3)}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Combine the fractions over common denominator $1 - \sin^2\theta$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** LHS $= \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{(1+\sin\theta) + (1-\sin\theta)}{(1-\sin\theta)(1+\sin\theta)}
= \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{2}{1-\sin^2\theta} = \dfrac{2\cos\theta}{\cos^2\theta} = \dfrac{2}{\cos\theta} = 2\sec\theta$. ✓

**(ii)** Using the identity, the value is $2\sec(\pi/3) = 2 \times 2 = 4$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 3 LA Q on "find all six trig
values given one" mirrors the 3-mark item.
**JEE pointer:** the $a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta$ trick (Stretch S2) is
JEE Main bread and butter.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Radians are just smaller degrees."** They are a different unit. A
   radian is *the* natural angle measure; calculus only behaves nicely
   for trig functions if you use radians.
2. **"$\sin^{-1}\theta$ means $1/\sin\theta$."** No. $\sin^{-1}$ is the
   inverse function (we meet it in Class 12). The reciprocal of $\sin$
   is $\csc$.
3. **"$\sin(A + B) = \sin A + \sin B$."** Definitely not. The compound
   angle formula is the topic of Lesson 3b.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you draw the unit circle and place the eight standard angles
   ($0, \pi/6, \pi/4, \pi/3, \pi/2, \ldots$) on it from memory?
2. Did you solve at least 6 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the
   7 practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you read off any trig value of any standard angle in any
   quadrant in under 5 seconds?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Trig Identities and Compound Angles](../03b-trig-identities-compound-angles/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** rebuild the unit circle and the special-angle table by
  hand five times. Speed comes from drilling.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Class 10 — Trigonometric Identities](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/08b-trigonometric-identities/index.qmd)** —
  the Pythagorean identity in the right-triangle setting.
- **[Unit Circle](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/unit-circle/index.qmd)** —
  the geometric picture extended here to all real angles.

### What this unlocks

- **[Compound Angle Identities](../03b-trig-identities-compound-angles/index.qmd)** —
  $\sin(A+B), \cos(A+B), \ldots$
- **[Limits](../12a-limits/index.qmd)** — the standard limit
  $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x} = 1$ requires radians.
- **[Class 12 — Inverse Trigonometric Functions](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/02-inverse-trigonometric-functions/index.qmd)**.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Functions** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised). Sections 3.1–3.3: angles, degree-radian, six trig
  functions, signs, domain and range, graphs.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`t3.trig-ratios`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/trig-ratios/index.qmd) and
  [`t3.unit-circle`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/unit-circle/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every rotation in a 2D game uses
$(x, y) \mapsto (x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta,\ x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta)$.
The unit circle is *the* tool for spinning sprites, aiming projectiles,
and orbiting cameras.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / Signal processing.** A sinusoid $A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ is
the building block of every periodic signal. Fourier showed that *any*
reasonable function is a sum of sines and cosines — a fact that powers
audio compression, MRI imaging, and wifi.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown (rotation as the unit-circle definition's
heart), Better Explained (radians as the natural angle), Russian
school (Discover sequence builds the table by force, not formula).
:::
